items.text: Chapter Nine
Symptoms, Damage, and Economic Losses Caused by Tuta absoluta
Diagnostic Symptoms, Effects of Infestation on the Plant and Yield, and Assessment of Economic Damage
Introduction
Tuta absoluta is one of the most serious insect pests of tomato because its larvae can attack most aerial parts of the plant from the seedling stage until fruit maturity.

Damage is not caused by the adult insect or the pupa, but by larvae that mine within plant tissues, feeding between the two leaf epidermal layers or entering growing points, stems, flowers, and fruits.
Infestation is particularly serious because larvae remain concealed within plant tissues, where the mines provide partial protection from:

items: Insecticides remaining on the external plant surface.

items.text: Infestation causes losses through two principal pathways:

items: 1. Direct losses resulting from tissue consumption and fruit damage.
2. Indirect losses resulting from pathogen entry, reduced plant efficiency, increased sorting and control costs, and crop rejection.

items.text: Losses may range from low levels that the plant can compensate for to near-total loss of marketable yield under severe infestation without effective management.
Difference Between Infestation, Damage, and Loss
Three basic terms must be distinguished.
Infestation
Infestation
This means the presence of the pest or any of its stages on the plant, such as:

items: Presence of adult moths in traps.

items.text: Infestation does not necessarily mean that an economic loss has occurred.
Damage
Damage
Damage is the visible or functional change caused by the insect in the plant, such as:
Economic Loss
Economic Loss
Economic loss is the reduction in crop value resulting from infestation and includes:

items: Rejection of a shipment or factory delivery.
Loss of part of the quality or shelf life.

items.text: Low leaf infestation may occur without a clear economic loss, whereas a single hole in a fresh-market tomato fruit may cause it to be rejected.
Stage Responsible for Damage
The larva is responsible for all direct damage.
Damage severity varies according to larval instar:

rows: Enlargement of mines and increased tissue consumption
Broad mines and movement into growing points, stems, and fruits
Highest tissue consumption and most severe damage to fruits and stems

items.text: Not all larvae are equally important. A young larva may be present without producing extensive damage, whereas an advanced larva may spoil more than one leaf or fruit by leaving one mine and moving to another site.
Initial Appearance of Symptoms
Infestation may begin in the nursery or immediately after transplanting.
The first symptoms commonly appear as:

items: A pale area between the two leaf epidermal layers.
A small larva visible when the leaf is held against the light.

items.text: Early infestation may pass unnoticed, particularly when:

items: Symptoms are confused with Liriomyza infestation.
Mines are not opened to confirm the presence of larvae.

items.text: Damage increases as larvae develop and generations accumulate.
Symptoms on Leaves
Leaves are the plant parts most frequently used for oviposition and the initial feeding of larvae.
Recent Leaf Mine
A mine begins as:
The larva feeds on mesophyll tissue between the two leaf epidermal layers, while the outer epidermis remains relatively intact during the early stage of infestation.
Advanced Mine
As feeding continues, the mine expands and becomes:

items: Gray or brown as it ages.

items.text: Frass may accumulate on one side of the mine or appear as dark lines and dots.
Old Mine
After the larva leaves or dies, the mine becomes:
Dead tissue may fall away, leaving irregular holes in the leaf.
Coalescence of Mines
At high pest density, mines formed by several larvae may overlap, converting a large part of the leaf into tissue that is:
Coalescence of mines causes loss of a large proportion of the effective green leaf area.
Leaf Curling and Desiccation
Severe damage may lead to:

items: Death of parts of the leaf.

items.text: Curling does not always occur in a regular pattern and may overlap with symptoms of water stress, viruses, or mites.
Effect of Leaf Infestation on Photosynthesis
Larvae feed on mesophyll cells, which contain a large proportion of the chloroplasts responsible for photosynthesis.
This results in:

items: Redirection of plant resources toward defensive responses.
Reduced root and shoot growth under severe infestation.

items.text: The effect is not limited to the visible mine area. Physiological and defensive responses may also occur in surrounding tissues.
Physiological studies have shown that infestation can reduce:
The magnitude of the reduction varies according to:

items: Ability of the plant to produce new leaves.

items.text: Ability of the Plant to Compensate for Leaf Damage
Not every level of leaf infestation causes a loss equal to the proportion of affected leaf area.
The plant may compensate for part of the damage through:
Compensatory capacity is greater when:

items: Fruits and growing points are not directly attacked.

items.text: Compensatory capacity decreases when:

items: Leaves close to fruit clusters are damaged.
Generations recur without reducing the population.
The plant is also affected by other pests or diseases.
The plant is exposed to water or salinity stress.

items.text: Symptoms on Growing Points
Larvae attack tender growing points and terminal and lateral buds.
Symptoms include:
Growing-point infestation is particularly serious in:
Death of the growing point in a young plant may cause:
Symptoms on Buds and Flowers
Larvae may infest:
Symptoms appear as:
Flowers are not the most common infestation site compared with leaves, but their infestation directly affects the number of fruits formed.
Symptoms on Leaf Petioles and Stems
Larvae enter petioles and tender stems, particularly near:
Signs include:

items: Wilting of the leaf beyond the infestation point.

items.text: The external tunnel may not be obvious, while wilting or weakening of the affected part becomes visible.
In seedlings, infestation of the main stem may cause:
Symptoms on Fruits
Fruit infestation represents the most important form of economic damage because infested fruits lose a large proportion of their fresh-market or processing value.
Larval Entry Sites
The larva may enter through:

items: The point where a leaf touches the fruit.
The point of contact between two fruits.

items.text: Infestation is common near the calyx because this area provides the larva with protection and a suitable hiding site.
Appearance of the Entry Hole
The entry hole is usually:

items: Less conspicuous than the internal damage.

items.text: The hole may be only a few millimeters in diameter while the tunnel extends through a large part of the fruit.
Tunnels Inside the Fruit
The larva feeds within:

items: The region close to the skin.

items.text: Tunnels appear as:
Exit Hole
After completing feeding, the larva may leave the fruit, resulting in:
More than one hole may occur in a single fruit because of movement by one larva or infestation by several larvae.
Infestation of Green and Ripe Fruits
Fruits may be infested as soon as they form.
Green Fruits
Symptoms include:

items: Premature fruit drop in some cases.

items.text: The fruit may continue growing while the damaged area expands or becomes invaded by rots.
Colored and Ripe Fruits
These are more prone to developing:
Infested fruits are difficult to store even when external damage appears limited.
Direct Damage
Direct damage caused by larval feeding includes:

items: Death of growing points and buds.

items.text: Direct fruit damage represents the largest component of loss because an infested fruit often cannot be sold as premium fresh produce.
Indirect Damage
Indirect damage results from effects following larval boring.
It includes:

items: Adverse effects on natural enemies due to repeated spraying.
Possible rejection of the crop or shipment.

items.text: The value of indirect damage may exceed the value of the tissue consumed directly by the larva.
Rots and Secondary Infections
Entry and exit holes and tunnels act as entry points for pathogens.
Wounds may permit entry of:

items: Microorganisms present on the plant surface or in soil.

items.text: Secondary infections lead to:

items: Spread of rot to contacting fruits.

items.text: Not all rotted tissue should be attributed to direct larval feeding. Damage may begin with a small opening and then multiply because of microbial activity.
Effect of Infestation on Plant Architecture
Under severe and repeated infestation, plant architecture changes because of:
Canopy collapse may lead to:

items: Reduced protection of fruits from weather conditions.

items.text: Tuta can therefore cause additional losses even in fruits not directly entered by larvae.
Effect of Infestation Timing
Loss varies according to the crop-growth stage.
Nursery Infestation
This causes:

items: Transport of the pest into the field.

items.text: Infestation After Transplanting
The plant may compensate for part of the leaf damage, but growing-point infestation may delay growth and reduce early yield.
Infestation During Flowering and Fruit Set
This affects:

items: Leaves responsible for supplying fruit clusters.

items.text: Infestation During Fruit Filling
This is a critical stage because leaf loss reduces carbohydrate supply to fruits, while larvae begin entering fruits.
Infestation Before Harvest
This may not greatly reduce the number of fruits formed, but it causes:
Losses in Fresh-Market Tomatoes
Fresh tomatoes are sold according to fruit appearance and surface integrity, so sensitivity to fruit infestation is extremely high.
A single hole may lead to:

items: Exclusion of the fruit from premium grade.
Increased risk of decay during transport.

items.text: Losses include:
The economic threshold for fruit infestation is therefore relatively low, especially for export and premium markets.
Losses in Processing Tomatoes
Acceptance of processing tomatoes does not depend on external appearance alone as it does in the fresh market, but Tuta infestation remains highly important.
Fruit infestation causes:

items: Reduced weight of sound fruits suitable for delivery.
Greater need for sorting and washing.
Reduced efficiency of harvesting and transport.
Possible rejection of a load when the proportion of infested fruits is high.

items.text: Two effects must be distinguished:
Leaf Damage Alone
The plant may compensate for low or moderate leaf infestation, and the presence of some mines does not automatically mean a reduction in °Brix or yield.
Fruit Infestation and Canopy Collapse
These cause direct losses in processing yield through:

There is no universal fixed numerical relationship between the proportion of leaf mines and °Brix because °Brix is affected by many factors, including cultivar, irrigation, maturity, crop load, and temperature.
Assessment of Tuta in processing tomatoes therefore depends mainly on:

items: Timing of infestation relative to harvest.

items.text: Magnitude of Recorded Losses
Losses vary greatly among countries, seasons, and production systems.
Reviews and reports indicate that actual losses may range under many conditions between approximately:
While in severe outbreaks without effective management they may reach:
80–100% of marketable yield.
A 100% loss does not mean that larvae consumed the entire crop biomass. It may mean that most fruits became:
Loss is affected by:
Factors Determining Loss Severity
Infestation Density
As the number of active larvae increases, so does the probability of:
Larval Instar
Third and fourth instars consume more tissue than young instars.
Duration of Infestation
A low infestation persisting for several weeks may be more serious than a short temporary peak.
Crop Stage
Plant compensatory capacity varies among growth stages.
Cultivar
Cultivars differ in:
Market Type
Loss is greater in crops intended for:
Some superficial defects may have less effect in processing, but holes and rots remain unacceptable.
Presence of Secondary Diseases
Rots multiply the value of damage resulting from mining.
Distinguishing Active from Old Infestation
The presence of a mine does not mean that a larva is still alive.
Active Mine
It is characterized by:

items: Larval response when the mine is opened.

items.text: Old Mine
It is characterized by:

items: Presence of cast skin or old frass.

items.text: Control decisions should be based on larvae, eggs, and active infestation rather than on the combined number of old and new mines.
Distinguishing Tuta from Liriomyza Leaf Miners

rows: On the plant or in soil
Usually outside the mine or in soil, depending on species

items.text: Mines may resemble one another when several infestations coalesce. Diagnosis must therefore depend on examination of the larva.
Distinguishing Tuta from Tomato Fruitworm

rows: Inside the mine or around the opening

items.text: Distinguishing Tuta Damage from Leaf Diseases
Old mines may be confused with:
A Tuta mine is usually characterized by:

items: A thin epidermis above the cavity.
Irregular boundaries following feeding within the tissue.

items.text: Disease lesions develop within the tissue without a larva or insect frass.
Estimating Plant Infestation Percentage
The percentage of infested plants is calculated as:
Infested plants (%) = Number of plants carrying active infestation ÷ Total number of plants examined × 100
Example:
If 100 plants are examined and active infestation is found on 18 plants:
Infestation percentage = 18 ÷ 100 × 100 = 18%
The definition of an infested plant must be specified, such as:
Different indicators should not be combined into one number without a clear definition.
Estimating Leaf Infestation
The following should be recorded for each plant:

items: Number of leaves bearing active mines.
Position of infestation on the plant.

items.text: The percentage of infested leaves is calculated as:
Infested leaves (%) = Number of infested leaves ÷ Number of leaves examined × 100
This indicator alone does not describe the volume of damage. One leaf may carry a small mine while another carries several broad mines.
The following should therefore be recorded with it:
Estimating Growing-Point Infestation
This is calculated as:
Infested growing points (%) = Number of plants with infested growing points ÷ Number of plants examined × 100
The type of infestation should be recorded:

items: Active growing point containing a larva.
Lateral growth resulting from death of the growing point.

items.text: Growing-point infestation is more important in young plants than in large plants.
Estimating Fruit Infestation
The percentage of infested fruits is one of the most important economic indicators.
It is calculated as:
Infested fruits (%) = Number of fruits carrying Tuta infestation ÷ Total number of fruits examined × 100
Weight loss is also calculated as:
Weight loss (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100
It is preferable to record separately:
The numerical percentage of infested fruits may differ from their percentage by weight.
Estimating Marketable Yield
Marketable yield is calculated as:
Marketable yield = Total yield − Weight of rejected fruits
Fruits rejected because of Tuta include:

The rejection rate differs according to market. A factory may accept a limited superficial defect that would be rejected by the fresh market, but it generally does not accept rotted fruits or fruits carrying live larvae.
Economic Injury Level and Economic Threshold
Economic Injury Level
Economic Injury Level
This is the lowest pest density at which the value of crop loss equals the cost of control.
Economic Threshold
Economic Threshold
This is the pest density at which intervention begins before the population reaches the economic injury level.
The economic threshold is lower than the economic injury level because control requires time before its effect becomes apparent.
Published Thresholds for Tuta absoluta
There is no single universal threshold applicable to all environments.
A study conducted in tomato greenhouses in Ethiopia determined:

items: Economic injury level: approximately 3 larvae per plant.
Economic threshold: approximately 2.25 larvae per plant.

items.text: Another field experiment estimated the economic threshold at approximately:
2.87 larvae per plant
And the economic injury level at approximately:
3.82 larvae per plant.
An Iranian study estimated economic injury levels between:
4.15 and 5.04 larvae per plant
Depending on the tomato cultivar.
These results demonstrate that the threshold varies according to:
Therefore, a value of 2.25 or 3 larvae per plant should not be used as a fixed rule for all Egyptian tomato fields.
Why Trap Counts Alone Are Insufficient to Determine Loss
Pheromone traps mainly attract males, whereas damage is caused by larvae originating from eggs laid by females.
Trap catches may be high without severe damage because of:

items: Immigration of males from a neighboring field.
Failure of part of the eggs to hatch.

items.text: Trap catches may be low while damage is high because of:

items: Large numbers of larvae already inside plants.

items.text: Traps must therefore be interpreted together with:
Field Decision Indicators
A field decision depends on several indicators, most importantly:

items: 1. Number of males captured and the weekly trend in catches.
3. Percentage of plants bearing active mines.
4. Number of live larvae per plant.

items.text: No single indicator provides a complete picture of risk.
Intervention Threshold in Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes
Fresh-Market Tomatoes
The threshold for fruit infestation is low because cosmetic damage directly affects price and acceptance.
Intervention begins before larvae reach fruits, based on eggs and recent mines.
Processing Tomatoes
The crop may tolerate a limited level of leaf mining more than fresh-market tomatoes, but fruit infestation should remain as low as possible.
The decision depends on:

items: Probability of larvae moving into fruits.

items.text: Intervention should not be delayed until the percentage of infested fruits increases because damage occurring inside the fruit cannot be repaired.
Assessment of Economic Loss
Total loss consists of:
Direct Yield Loss
Control Costs
Postharvest Costs
Indirect Losses

items: Loss of a supply contract or market.

items.text: Simplified Calculation Model
If total yield is:
40 metric tons per feddan
And the percentage of fruits rejected because of Tuta is:
Direct loss equals:
40 × 0.08 = 3.2 metric tons per feddan
If the price per metric ton is:
The value of direct loss is:
3.2 × 5,000 = EGP 16,000 per feddan
The following must then be added:

items: Any deductions applied by the buyer.

items.text: This example does not include the effect of reduced yield resulting from leaf or growing-point loss.
Preventing Infestation from Becoming a Loss
The difference between limited infestation and severe loss depends on the speed of intervention and program efficiency.
Loss prevention depends on:

items: Preventing L3 and L4 from reaching fruits.
Removing heavily infested growing points and leaves when possible.
Managing old fields and crop residues.
Applying agricultural sanitation after the season ends.

items.text: Symptom-Diagnosis Table

rows: Wilting and death of the bud
Weakness or death of the upper part
Loss of leaves and growing points

items.text: Chapter Summary
Larvae of Tuta absoluta cause direct plant damage, whereas the adult moth performs the functions of reproduction and dispersal.
Infestation usually begins with small leaf mines that expand as larvae grow. At high pest density, mines coalesce, tissues dry, and leaves lose a large part of their photosynthetic capacity.

Larvae also attack growing points, buds, stems, flowers, and fruits. Growing-point infestation causes growth deformation, whereas fruit infestation represents the principal component of economic loss.
Holes and tunnels permit entry of rot pathogens, allowing a small opening to develop into a large area of decomposed tissue.
Losses vary according to infestation severity and timing, cultivar, market, and production system, and may range from low levels to 80–100% loss of marketable yield in severe unmanaged cases.
The presence of an old mine does not necessarily require treatment. Decisions should be based on eggs, active mines, live larvae, and growing-point and fruit infestation.

Some studies estimated the economic threshold at approximately 2.25–2.87 larvae per plant, while others estimated the economic injury level at approximately 3–5 larvae per plant depending on cultivar and environment.
These values are not fixed universal thresholds because the threshold changes with crop price, control cost and efficacy, and market type.
The percentage of infested fruits is the most important economic indicator, particularly in fresh-market and processing tomatoes intended for delivery.

Successful management depends on preventing infestation from moving from leaves into growing points, stems, and fruits and targeting eggs and young larvae before infestation becomes an irreversible loss.
